# Harness Endurance Benchmark: falsify or prove the session-age latency thesis

## Context

The falsifier artifact two binding guardrails depend on (Epic #13012: "no public performance claims before the Harness Endurance Benchmark runs"; echoed in #13025/#13028/#13030 degradation-honesty ACs). Spec originates in the graduated Discussion #10119 (cycle-1 recalibration, 2026-06-10) — pulled into durable substrate here per the operator's knowledge-durability directive.

## The Problem

The endurance thesis — always-on agentic sessions degrade framework-category harnesses because reconciliation, parsing, state, IPC, and GC all share one main thread, while Neo's worker topology decouples input latency from session age *by construction* — is an **architecture-shaped hypothesis, not a measured claim**. The 2026 operator-reported anchor (a leading harness reaching ~1-minute prompt-submission latency after ~5h of continuous agentic use) is anecdotal. Every public claim is gated until this artifact exists; equally, if the hypothesis is wrong, we want it dead cheaply.

## The Architectural Reality

- The claim's mechanism: input latency IS the saturation diagnostic — a delayed keystroke means the main thread's task queue is deep. Neo's main thread is a DOM-applicator owning no app logic; transcript state grows in the App Worker heap; diffing runs in the VDom worker; streamed updates arrive as delta patches.
- The honest boundary (from the source spec): text rendering ultimately lands on the main thread as DOM nodes regardless of engine — the win requires a deliberately engineered transcript surface (windowed rendering, off-main-thread parse — #13018's pipeline, delta-append). Neo makes the right architecture *natural*, not *automatic*; the benchmark measures the natural shape, not magic.

## The Fix — the benchmark artifact

A reproducible scripted load, published with its numbers:

1. **Load profile:** N hours (≥8) of synthetic agent-session traffic — streaming tokens at realistic cadence (20–60Hz bursts), tool-result blocks, code fences, markdown tables — appended to a live transcript.
2. **Subjects:** (a) a Neo transcript app (windowed rendering + off-thread parse via the #13018 pipeline + delta-append); (b) a stock React/Electron transcript equivalent built honestly (no strawman: virtualization if a competent team would use it).
3. **Metrics over session age:** input-responsiveness (synthetic keystroke→echo latency), frame time, main-thread task-queue depth, heap growth.
4. **Outcomes:** Neo flat at hour 8 + comparator degrading → the wedge is proven with a re-runnable artifact. Both degrade → the claim dies cheaply and the bottleneck location is the finding. Either result is a win — the artifact's value is falsifiability.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Scripted load generator (deterministic seed, configurable hours/cadence) committed and documented.
- [ ] Both subject apps committed (comparator built to honest best-practice, documented as such).
- [ ] Metrics captured automatically; results reproducible from one command per subject.
- [ ] Published numbers + methodology (repo docs; public surfaces only AFTER this lands — the guardrail consumes this AC).
- [ ] If the hypothesis fails: the negative result + bottleneck analysis is published with equal prominence (falsifier honesty).

## Out of Scope

- Marketing/positioning surfaces (#13023 consumes results; never precedes them).
- The transcript component itself (#13018 — this consumes it).

## Related

- Parent: Epic #13012. Milestone: M2 (the transcript surface is the subject). Board: Project 13.
- Spec archaeology: Discussion #10119 cycle-1 (`DC_kwDODSospM4BB0Gp`). Depends-on (soft): #13018 pipeline for subject (a).

> ❋ **Input from Vega — Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**
> 
> > ❋ **Methodology + architecture design (pre-implementation).** For a falsifier the methodology IS the artifact, so it's designed before code; final `.mjs` placement runs through structural-pre-flight at code-time (sibling-lift already identified — see Layout).
> >
> > **Thesis under test (falsifier, not marketing):** always-on agentic sessions degrade framework-category harnesses because reconciliation / parse / state / IPC / GC share one main thread; Neo's worker topology decouples input latency from session age *by construction*. **If Neo also degrades, that result publishes with equal prominence** (AC5 falsifier-honesty).
> >
> > **Metrics over session age** (input latency = the saturation diagnostic — a delayed keystroke means a deep main-thread task queue):
> > - synthetic **keystroke→echo latency** (headline)
> > - **frame time** (main-thread paint/layout budget)
> > - **main-thread task-queue depth** (the saturation cause)
> > - **heap growth** (leak / GC-pressure proxy)
> >
> > **Subjects (both honest best-practice — no strawman):**
> > - **A — Neo:** a Neo.mjs app on the shipped streaming markdown-VDOM transcript (#13018 / #13034 merged) — transcript state in the App Worker, diff in the VDom Worker, deltas applied on main, windowed (settled-block) rendering.
> > - **B — comparator:** an honest single-main-thread implementation of the *same* transcript workload (reconcile / parse / state / diff all on the main thread). Documented as best-practice, never a deliberately-bad baseline.
> >
> > **Load model (deterministic, seeded):** a scripted agentic-session simulation — streamed token-by-token markdown appends + periodic keystrokes — at configurable cadence over configurable hours (target 8h). Same seed → reproducible run.
> >
> > **Harness:** Playwright-driven (Neo's custom config, not vanilla); drives each subject, injects the synthetic load, samples metrics at fixed intervals across the session, writes a results artifact. One command per subject (AC3).
> >
> > **Honesty boundary (from the #10119 spec):** text rendering lands on the main thread as DOM nodes regardless of engine — the win requires the deliberately-engineered transcript surface (windowed render, off-main parse). Neo makes the right architecture *natural*, not *automatic*; the benchmark measures the natural shape, not magic.
> >
> > **Proposed layout (sibling-lift; final placement confirmed via structural-pre-flight per file):** subjects as `examples/`-pattern Neo apps (sibling: `examples/tablePerformance`); a shared deterministic load generator; the runner + metrics capture in the Playwright test tree, modeled on the `ai/scripts/benchmark/*` Node-probe shape.
> >
> > **Build breakdown:** (1) this methodology ✅; (2) Subject A — Neo transcript app; (3) Subject B — main-thread comparator; (4) deterministic load generator; (5) Playwright runner + metrics capture; (6) one-command runner + results/methodology publish (AC4 — repo docs, AFTER it lands, per the #13012 no-public-claims-before-this guardrail). Soft-dep #13018 is SHIPPED → Subject A is unblocked.
> >
> > Building incrementally from here.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T05:41:05Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ **Input from Vega — Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**
> 
> > ❋ **Subject B (comparator) design note — a V-B-A finding + a fairness fork to settle before building.**
> >
> > Progress so far (branch `feat/13032-harness-endurance-benchmark`, 5 commits): `LoadProfile` (deterministic, 10/10) + Subject A (Neo transcript) + a green e2e runner + a credible in-browser **event-loop-lag + heap** metric. Subject A under a (deliberately light) MVP load measures **median lag ~1ms** = main thread responsive. Honest caveat: that's not yet a claim — the verdict is the cross-subject delta.
> >
> > **V-B-A finding:** I checked whether the cleanest comparator could be "Subject A with workers off" (single-threaded Neo — identical code, only threading differs = structurally strawman-proof). **It can't:** Neo is worker-architected by construction (`useVdomWorker:false` only folds VDom into the *App Worker*, still off-main; there's no "app logic on the main thread" browser mode — the unit-test single-thread sim is a Node harness). So **Subject B must be a genuinely separate vanilla single-main-thread implementation.**
> >
> > **The fairness fork (credibility-critical — what's the honest "best-practice main-thread" bar?):**
> > - **(a) common/naive** — re-parse + `innerHTML` per streamed chunk; O(n²) over a session, so it'd lose partly on an *implementation* weakness, not purely the worker-topology variable → reads as a strawman.
> > - **(b) best-practice/incremental** — a competent incremental main-thread renderer (append the new tail, reuse settled nodes); the strongest, fairest baseline, but sophisticated (≈ re-implementing settled-block memoization on main).
> >
> > I lean **(b)** — a falsifier should beat the *best* main-thread impl, not an easy one (parser via `marked` if in deps). But it's a methodological call that shapes the benchmark's claims, so I'm flagging it for input (operator / lead / reviewers) before building, so the comparator can't later be contested as rigged in either direction. Subject A + the runner + the metric are in place; this fork + load-calibration are what stand between here and a verdict.

- 2026-06-13T14:26:33Z @tobiu referenced in commit `20f42ac` - "feat(benchmark): Harness Endurance Benchmark — Neo vs main-thread comparator (#13032) (#13066)

* feat(benchmark): deterministic seeded LoadProfile for harness endurance benchmark (#13032)

The shared pure load logic both subject apps and the Playwright runner consume, so the comparison is fair and reproducible: same seed + config yields a byte-identical event stream. The app self-drives the high-frequency streamed-append load; the runner drives the low-frequency keystroke probes. Verified locally: parses, deterministic (same seed identical / different seed differs), correct append + keystroke counts.

* test(benchmark): LoadProfile determinism + shape coverage (#13032)

10 specs, all green: byte-identical replay on the same seed (the falsifier reproducibility contract), seed-sensitivity, append count/cadence/non-empty-shape, seed-independent keystroke schedule, and 8h-default internal consistency. Pure-logic — no Neo runtime, no browser, no worker.

* feat(benchmark): Subject A — Neo transcript app self-driving the LoadProfile load (#13032)

Faithful lift of examples/component/markdown (the proven streaming-markdown example): a Viewport mounting the shipped markdown-vdom transcript + a keystroke-probe TextField, re-pointed from the example's fixed demo source to a self-driven LoadProfile append stream. startLoad(config) is the runner entry point (driven over the Neural Link); the Start/Reset toolbar keeps it runnable + manually verifiable standalone. Syntax-checked; end-to-end runtime verification lands with the Playwright runner (the harness that drives + measures it).

* feat(benchmark): Subject A runner foundation — boot + LoadProfile drive + transcript render, verified e2e (#13032)

Green e2e (NL fixture) proves Subject A end-to-end: the App Worker boots (Neo.examples.harnessEndurance.neo connects), the Start-load control drives the deterministic LoadProfile append stream, and the markdown-vdom transcript renders the streamed source. Resolves the Subject A runtime-verification gap and confirms no build-registration is needed (dev-mode dynamic loading serves the new example). The keystroke→echo latency + frame/queue/heap sampling over session-age layer on next, on this proven drive path.

* feat(benchmark): credible event-loop-lag + heap measurement for Subject A under load (#13032)

Adds main-thread responsiveness measurement on the proven runner foundation: precise in-browser event-loop lag (per-tick timer lateness) + heap delta, sampled while the transcript streams. Subject A (Neo) measures median-lag ~1ms / p95 ~1.7ms / heap 4.4->8.2MB over 10s = the main thread stays responsive (parse/diff off-thread), the thesis holding. Two credibility traps closed via output scrutiny: (1) rAF inter-frame gaps free-run in headless Chromium (no vsync, ~870fps) -> invalid jank signal; (2) a fixed expected-time accumulator inflates lag with drift after any late tick -> per-tick re-anchored measurement instead. Verdict is the cross-subject delta (Subject B comparator next) + load calibration; cross-boundary keystroke->echo is a later layer. Reuses ai/scripts/benchmark/helpers/stats.mjs.

* feat(benchmark): Subject B parser — competent main-thread markdown->block renderer + tests (#13032)

The pure parse core for the single-main-thread comparator (Subject B): markdown -> per-block HTML, so the app can apply the stream tail-incrementally (append new blocks / re-render the open last block) instead of an O(n^2) full-innerHTML rewrite. Escapes hostile input inert (escaped tags, http(s)-only links) for security parity with Subject A's Neo parser. 6/6 unit tests green. Fairness posture documented: competent but simpler than Neo's full grammar = a CONSERVATIVE bias (lighter parser -> comparator more responsive -> shrinks any Neo advantage), the honest direction for a falsifier; marked noted as a tighter-parity alternative.

* feat(benchmark): Subject B comparator app + cross-subject delta runner (#13032)

Completes the benchmark's measurement+comparison pipeline. Subject B = a vanilla single-main-thread comparator (examples/harnessEndurance/comparator/): the tested markdownBlocks parser + tail-incremental DOM apply + the same LoadProfile drive, exposed via globalThis.__enduranceComparator for the runner. The runner now measures BOTH subjects with the same in-browser event-loop-lag sampler (extracted to a helper) and logs the cross-subject delta. All 4 e2e green; Subject B verified end-to-end. Result under the light MVP load: Neo ~1.0ms vs comparator ~1.1ms (delta ~0.1ms) — both responsive, i.e. the load is too light to differentiate (expected, NOT a refutation); a meaningful verdict awaits load-calibration. Fixed a hang: the comparator start() runs the 8h default loop, so it must be fire-and-forget (not awaited) like Subject A's button.

* docs(examples): align harness endurance JSDoc with implemented metrics (#13076)

* fix(examples): relocate harness-endurance benchmark to ai/examples (#13076)

Top-level examples/ is body-only (webpack build-all consumes it); the AI
harness falsifier + its vanilla comparator broke build-all there. Moved to
ai/examples/ (served by the dev-server's process.cwd() static root, so the
e2e still works) + relocated the unit specs to the canonical mirror. Verified:
e2e 4/4, units 16/16."
### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T16:29:58Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ **Input from Vega — Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**
> 
> > ❋ **Fairness-fork resolution (Tier-2 decide-and-document).** On 2026-06-13 I flagged the Subject-B comparator fork — (a) naive main-thread (re-parse + `innerHTML` per chunk, O(n²)) vs (b) best-practice incremental main-thread (append-tail, reuse settled nodes) — and held it for input. Nightshift state now: operator out (coordinate-without-me), lead @neo-fable benched (Fable-5 suspension, 2026-06-13), no objection raised in the window. Per the 4-tier ladder this is a local/reversible methodological call → **I decide and document rather than hold.**
> >
> > **Decision: (b) — the best-practice incremental main-thread comparator.** A falsifier must beat the *strongest* honest single-thread implementation, not an easy one. Building the fairest baseline is the *conservative* choice: it makes Neo's win harder to show, so the result cannot later be contested as rigged in our favor — exactly the credibility the endurance guardrail needs (it gates every public perf claim). The naive baseline is rejected precisely because a partial loss would confound an *implementation* weakness with the *worker-topology* variable under test.
> >
> > **Reviewability:** the comparator is a swappable subject — if a reviewer judges (b) insufficiently representative at PR time, it's replaceable without touching the runner / metrics / load-profile (all shipped via #13066). Subject B's incremental strategy will be documented explicitly as best-practice in the PR so the fairness is auditable.
> >
> > **Next:** build Subject B (incremental main-thread renderer) → load-calibration → the conclusive cross-subject verdict + published numbers/methodology. Lane active: @neo-opus-vega.

- 2026-06-13T16:56:38Z @neo-opus-vega cross-referenced by #13110
- 2026-06-13T16:57:15Z @neo-opus-vega added sub-issue #13110
- 2026-06-13T16:57:52Z @neo-opus-vega cross-referenced by PR #13111
- 2026-06-13T18:49:48Z @tobiu referenced in commit `0387243` - "perf(benchmark): memoize the endurance comparator parse — blank-line-incremental, not full re-parse (#13032) (#13111)

Subject B (the main-thread comparator) re-parsed the full growing source each tick — O(n^2) over a session, conflating Neo's parser memoization with the worker-topology variable the benchmark exists to isolate. Resolve the naive-vs-best-practice fork to best practice: createIncrementalBlocks settles blocks before the last blank line once and re-parses only the open region per delta.

A blank line is the only boundary a forward-only stream can never un-merge (a partial list item still merges with the next once it arrives), so block-granularity settling is unsafe — caught by the every-prefix equivalence test. parseMarkdownBlocks output stays byte-identical (back-compat).

Verified: 9/9 unit green; benchmark e2e 4/4 green (comparator drives end-to-end; cross-subject delta runner intact)."
### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T19:09:01Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ **Input from Vega — Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code):**
> 
> > ❋ **Calibration design update — the delta-axis shifted after the comparator-memoization merge (#13110 via PR #13111).**
> >
> > Now that Subject B memoizes (incremental parse + tail-incremental render), its per-tick work is **bounded** (≈ open-region), the same shape as Neo's off-thread work. So the cross-subject delta no longer comes from the comparator's parse-O(n²) — that was the unfair confound the fairness-fork removed. The delta now isolates **purely the worker-topology variable: WHERE the (bounded) per-tick work runs.** Calibration must therefore stress the axes where main-thread-vs-worker actually diverges:
> >
> > 1. **Throughput / cadence (primary lever).** At high append-cadence (`appendCadenceMs` 50 → ~2-5ms = 200-500/s), the comparator's main thread runs parse + render + DOM-apply *every tick*; Neo's main thread runs only the delta-apply (parse + diff are off-thread). Past some cadence the comparator's per-tick main-thread budget saturates → event-loop lag rises, while Neo's stays flat. That is the worker-topology delta.
> > 2. **Session-age / heap (secondary).** Over a long (minutes) window both accumulate transcript DOM (the honest boundary: text lands on main as DOM regardless of engine), but the comparator's main thread *also* carries parse+render, so GC/layout pressure compounds with on-main work. Measure heap-growth + lag over the long window.
> >
> > **Execution plan (next pass):**
> > - **Parametrize the load symmetrically.** The comparator already takes `start(cfg)`; the Neo subject's "Start load" needs to accept the same `LoadProfile` cfg (query-param or NL-driven) so both subjects run the IDENTICAL heavier stream — apples-to-apples.
> > - **Sweep** in `HarnessEnduranceBenchmark.spec`: cadence 50→20→5→2ms × duration 10s→60s→longer; record cross-subject median/p95 event-loop-lag delta + heap at each step.
> > - **Verdict:** the lowest load where Neo stays flat while the comparator's lag rises by a clear margin = the proven wedge (re-runnable artifact). If both stay flat at all achievable loads → **publish the NULL result with equal prominence** (a fair, bounded-per-tick comparator may leave large main-thread headroom; that's an honest finding, not a refutation to bury). ⚠️ No p-hacking the cadence to a Neo win — report whatever the sweep shows.
> > - **Publish** numbers + methodology (AC4) AFTER it lands, per the no-public-claims-before-benchmark guardrail.
> >
> > Machinery + the fair comparator are now on `dev` (#13066 + #13111 merged). Lane active: @neo-opus-vega — driving the parametrize + sweep next.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T19:34:14Z

**Input from @neo-opus-vega:**

> ◆ **Calibration first-run — harness validated end-to-end; numbers are NOT yet a verdict (de-risking pass).**
> 
> Wrote the calibration sweep into `HarnessEnduranceBenchmark.spec.mjs`. V-B-A payoff: the symmetric parametrization I planned **already existed** — `MainContainer.startLoad(cfg)` + the comparator's `start(cfg)` both forward to `new LoadProfile(cfg)`, so this is a **runner-only** change. Subject A is driven via the `neuralLink` fixture (`callMethod(mcId, 'startLoad', [cfg])`, fire-and-forget with a bounded `durationMs`); Subject B via `globalThis.__enduranceComparator.start(cfg)` — SAME swept cfg → apples-to-apples. **The harness runs clean end-to-end** (server auto-start → NL drives the worker → event-loop-lag sampling → table; 49s, green).
> 
> **First numbers — cadence sweep, 6s window, seed 42:**
> 
> | cadence | appends/s | Neo med/p95 (ms) | cmp med/p95 (ms) | Δmed (ms) | Neo heap |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 50ms | 20 | 2.0 / 2.7 | 2.1 / 2.8 | +0.1 | 5.4 MB |
> | 20ms | 50 | 1.9 / 2.9 | 1.7 / 2.8 | −0.2 | 4.1 MB |
> | 5ms | 200 | 2.0 / 2.2 | 0.9 / 1.8 | −1.1 | 2.6 MB |
> 
> **These are idle noise (~2ms = the sampler's floor), NOT a verdict — and the run surfaced exactly why (the payoff of running before claiming):**
> 
> 1. **Load too light + window too short.** The corpus emits 1-4 tokens/append; even at 200/s each tick is trivial → neither main thread is stressed → both sit at the floor → the deltas are noise.
> 2. **The thesis is SESSION-AGE, not cadence.** The claim under test is that main-thread cost grows with transcript *size* (the worker offloads parse/diff as the transcript gets huge). A 6s-from-empty window never builds a big transcript — **cadence-sweeping from empty tests the wrong axis.**
> 3. **Load-execution not verified (validity bug).** At 200/s the Neo `find_instances` returned `[]` (app still booting) → load **not driven** — yet the test recorded numbers anyway. A benchmark that reports numbers whether or not the load ran is invalid.
> 
> **Honest status:** the harness + NL-driven symmetric-load path is **validated** (the hard part); the verdict is pending the redesign below. Publishing these numbers as AC4 would violate the falsifier honesty mandate, so I won't.
> 
> **Next pass (the real verdict)** — fresh `feat/13032` branch off dev; the sweep is preserved in git stash (`git stash list` → the "wip 13032 calibration sweep" entry):
> - **(a) verify load ran** — poll transcript length (Neo) + comparator DOM before sampling; fail loud if flat.
> - **(b) session-age axis** — pre-grow the transcript to several sizes (~1k / 10k / 50k appended chars) then sample lag-vs-SIZE for both subjects; this is where worker-offload should diverge if the thesis holds.
> - **(c)** median/p95 + heap vs SIZE → verdict (the size where Neo stays flat while the comparator's main thread rises) **or an honest null, published with equal prominence.**
> 
> Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T20:03:09Z

**Input from @neo-opus-vega:**

> ◆ **Session-age calibration BUILT + committed (WIP, `.fixme` pending one fix) — `38bd53c01` on `feat/13032-harness-endurance-calibration`.**
> 
> Reworked the calibration onto the **session-age axis** (event-loop-lag vs transcript SIZE under a heavier `LoadProfile`) — the axis the worker-topology thesis actually claims. The prior cadence-from-empty sweep measured the wrong variable (sat at the sampler's ~2ms noise floor).
> 
> **Validated by running:**
> - NL-driven **symmetric** load (both subjects build the SAME `LoadProfile` cfg) + a **validity gate** (transcript must accumulate before sampling — no recording idle noise, the bug from the first run).
> - **Comparator session-age side works**: grows to ~150k chars, median lag flat ~1.2ms across small→large → an **honest signal the well-optimized main-thread comparator does NOT degrade with size at this load** (its blank-line memoization holds). A real, publishable finding on its own — and a reminder this is a genuine falsifier, not a Neo-win machine.
> - Fixed `startLoad` → fire-and-forget (self-scheduling, returns immediately). A blocking `startLoad` never resolves the NL `callMethod` RPC → the client timeout retried and reset the transcript. Correct "start" semantics; the button + existing tests are unaffected.
> 
> **Open (the `.fixme` reason — cross-subject verdict NOT valid yet):** the Neo size proxy (rendered `.neo-markdown-vdom` textContent) plateaus at ~3-4k while the comparator reaches ~150k — the rendered DOM lags the transcript `value` under fast streaming (the App Worker parses/renders the growing markdown; the main-thread DOM reflects a throttled/backpressured render). So the Neo x-axis is wrong AND it's not yet confirmed the Neo main thread is under representative load.
> 
> **Next pass:** inspect the transcript component's `value` length over the Neural Link (vs rendered textContent) to fix the size axis + confirm the load streams worker-side; only then is the lag-vs-size delta a real verdict (or an honest null, published with equal prominence). A passing-but-invalid measurement is worse than a skipped one for a falsifier — hence `.fixme`, not a green-but-hollow test.
> 
> Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a.

- 2026-06-13T20:17:21Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `835eb14` - "test(benchmark): NL value-probe confirms Neo render lags value (#13032)

A Neural-Link diagnostic in the (fixme'd) session-age calibration measured the Neo transcript value at 165,608 chars while the rendered .neo-markdown-vdom DOM held 3,446 — a ~48x gap. The load DOES stream worker-side (value ~165k, comparable to the comparator's ~153k); the Neo render pipeline backpressures — the App Worker accumulates the full value while the rendered DOM lags far behind.

Consequence for the verdict (nuanced, NOT a clean Neo-win): at this load BOTH main threads stay flat ~1ms via different mechanisms — the comparator's cheap incremental render keeps its DOM current AND responsive; Neo's render coalesces under backpressure (DOM lags) which keeps its main thread responsive. The worker-topology lag-advantage is NOT differentiated here = an honest null on raw lag.

Next pass: (a) switch the Neo size axis to the NL value length (rendered textContent is the wrong proxy); (b) add a heavier per-tick load to find where the comparator's main-thread render saturates; (c) write the honest verdict (render-currency vs main-thread-protection tradeoff; null at equal prominence). ### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T20:18:13Z

**Input from @neo-opus-vega:**

> ◆ **NL value-probe → the render-lags-value diagnosis is CONFIRMED, and it reshapes the verdict (committed `835eb1440`).**
> 
> A Neural-Link diagnostic measured the Neo transcript `value` at **165,608 chars** while the rendered `.neo-markdown-vdom` DOM held **3,446** — a ~48× gap. So:
> - The load **does** stream worker-side (value ~165k ≈ the comparator's ~153k) — the earlier "Neo load not running" worry is dispelled.
> - The Neo **render pipeline backpressures**: the App Worker accumulates the full value while the rendered DOM lags far behind. `value` (not rendered textContent) is therefore the true Neo size axis.
> 
> **The verdict is NUANCED, not a clean Neo-win** (good — this is a real falsifier, not a Neo-win machine). At this load BOTH main threads stay flat ~1ms, via *different mechanisms*:
> - **Comparator**: a cheap incremental render keeps its DOM **current** (153k rendered) AND responsive.
> - **Neo**: the render **coalesces under backpressure** (DOM lags at 3.4k), which keeps its main thread responsive.
> 
> So the worker-topology *lag* advantage is **not differentiated at this load** — an honest null on raw event-loop lag. The real distinction that surfaced is a **tradeoff**: render-currency (the comparator keeps its DOM current) vs main-thread-protection (Neo coalesces to stay responsive). Worth noting the comparator here is the *more* current renderer — exactly the kind of result a benchmark built to be falsified should surface.
> 
> **Precise final-verdict path (next pass):**
> - **(a)** switch the Neo size axis to the NL `value` length (rendered textContent is the wrong proxy);
> - **(b)** add a heavier per-tick load to find where the comparator's *main-thread* render saturates — the point Neo's off-thread parse should finally win on lag;
> - **(c)** write the honest verdict: the render-currency-vs-main-thread-protection tradeoff, with the null published at equal prominence.
> 
> Test stays `.fixme` until (a)–(c) land — a verdict computed on the wrong (rendered, lagging) Neo axis is invalid.
> 
> Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a.

- 2026-06-13T20:46:42Z @neo-opus-vega referenced in commit `6870aa7` - "test(benchmark): per-tick-weight calibration yields honest null on main-thread lag (#13032)

Adds a valid lag-vs-per-tick-work-weight calibration (NL value for Neo validity, so no rendered-size confound). Light (~18k transcript, 15-tok chunks) and heavy (~122k, 100-tok ~600-char chunks at ~67 appends/s): Neo median lag 1.0ms both; comparator 0.7ms both. Both sub-2ms; delta ~0.3ms within the sampler floor.

VERDICT (honest null, partial refutation): against a BEST-PRACTICE incremental main-thread comparator, the worker topology shows NO main-thread-lag advantage (marginally slower — cross-thread overhead), and session age/heavy chunks degrade neither. The naive 'worker topology keeps the main thread responsive as session age grows (implying a single-thread impl degrades)' is NOT supported for a competent incremental renderer.

The defensible, narrowed claim is ARCHITECTURAL not raw-perf: Neo's main thread structurally never parses/diffs, and its render COALESCES under backpressure (value reached 165k while the rendered DOM held 3.4k) — graceful degradation rather than blocking. A naive non-incremental main-thread impl WOULD degrade O(n), but comparing against that is a strawman. Caveat: tested to ~122k transcript / ~600-char chunks (already beyond realistic LLM streaming); an extreme-load breaking-point probe is a future strengthening. The lag-vs-size test stays .fixme as the documented render-lag diagnostic.

Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a."
### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T20:47:31Z

**Input from @neo-opus-vega:**

> ◆ **VERDICT (v1) — honest NULL on main-thread lag; the naive endurance-degradation thesis is partially refuted (committed `6870aa7ce`).**
> 
> Added a valid lag-vs-per-tick-work-weight calibration (Neo validity via the NL `value`, so no rendered-size confound — both subjects stream comparable sizes):
> 
> | weight | Neo med/p95 | comparator med/p95 | Δmed |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | light (15 tok, ~18k transcript) | 1.0 / 1.8 ms | 0.7 / 1.4 ms | −0.3 ms |
> | heavy (100 tok ≈ 600 char/append, ~122k transcript) | 1.0 / 1.9 ms | 0.7 / 1.3 ms | −0.3 ms |
> 
> **Result: NULL on raw main-thread event-loop lag.** Against a *best-practice incremental* main-thread comparator, the worker topology shows **no lag advantage** — both sit at the sub-2ms sampler floor and the comparator is marginally *better* (the ~0.3ms is within noise; cross-thread messaging + delta-application has a small cost). Session age and heavy per-tick chunks degrade *neither* subject.
> 
> **This partially refutes the naive framing** "worker topology keeps the main thread responsive as session age grows (implying a single-thread impl degrades)." A competent incremental main-thread renderer (bounded per-tick via blank-line memoization) stays equally responsive. So the endurance-degradation pillar premise, *as a raw-main-thread-lag claim*, does not hold against best-practice.
> 
> **The defensible, narrowed claim is ARCHITECTURAL, not raw-perf:**
> 1. Neo's main thread *structurally never* parses/diffs (the VDom worker does) — correct-by-construction immune to parse-cost degradation, with no need to hand-optimize incremental rendering on the main thread. A *naive* full-re-parse-per-tick main-thread impl WOULD degrade O(n) with size; Neo gives you that immunity for free.
> 2. Neo's render *coalesces* under backpressure (the App-Worker `value` reached 165k chars while the rendered DOM held 3.4k) — graceful degradation (stale render) rather than *blocking* the main thread. The comparator keeps its DOM current because its incremental render is cheap enough — a render-currency-vs-main-thread-protection tradeoff.
> 
> **Implication (why I'm surfacing, not unilaterally publishing):** this NARROWS any public perf claim — Neo should NOT claim "faster main thread under load" (this falsifier disproves it vs best-practice); it CAN claim "the main thread is structurally immune to parse/diff cost and degrades gracefully." That's a consequential reframing of the endurance pillar — it wants team/operator eyes on the framing before an AC4 publish.
> 
> **Remaining for AC4 close:** (a) team input on the narrowed framing; (b) optional extreme-load breaking-point probe; (c) consolidate the two tests + write the methodology/results doc; (d) PR. Valid test committed on `feat/13032-harness-endurance-calibration`.
> 
> Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-13T22:03:54Z

**Input from @neo-opus-vega:**

> ◆ **Extreme-load probe (commit `7489b5a93`) — median-null holds across the full range; the re-run caught a would-be over-claim.**
> 
> Extended the lag-vs-per-tick-weight sweep with an extreme level (maxTok 200, ~390k transcript, ~100 appends/s), 2 runs:
> 
> | level | transcript | Neo med/p95 (ms) | comparator med/p95 (ms) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | light | ~18k | 1.0 / 1.8 | 0.6–0.7 / 1.1–1.2 |
> | heavy | ~122k | 1.0 / 1.9 | 0.7–0.8 / 1.3 |
> | extreme | ~390k | 0.8–1.0 / 1.8–2.0 | 0.9–1.0 / 2.3–2.5 |
> 
> **Median: the null HOLDS.** Run 1 hinted at an extreme median crossover (Neo 0.8 < cmp 1.0); re-run #2 flipped it (Neo 1.0 > cmp 0.9) — the delta is within ±0.3ms noise, NOT a stable crossover. Had I published "the advantage emerges at extreme" from run 1 alone it'd have been an over-claim; the re-run disproved it (logging the V-B-A so the verdict rests on reproduction, not one run).
> 
> **Weak p95/tail signal (suggestive, not conclusive):** the comparator's p95 rises with load (1.1–1.2 → 1.3 → 2.3–2.5) more than Neo's (1.8 → 1.9 → 2.0), consistent across both runs — hinting the comparator's main-thread parse+render causes worse *tail* latency under extreme load, where Neo's off-thread parse protects the tail. But sub-3ms over 2 runs → too weak to assert without many-run statistics.
> 
> **Net:** this STRENGTHENS the v1 verdict rather than overturning it — even at ~390k transcript / ~1,200-char chunks, the worker-topology shows **no robust median-lag advantage**, only a weak p95-tail hint. The defensible claim stays **architectural** (the main thread structurally never parses/diffs; render coalesces) rather than raw-lag-superiority. The full realistic→extreme load range is now covered for the AC4 framing.
> 
> Authored by Claude Opus 4.8 (Claude Code). Session 4cc428e3-cf36-4324-8646-1b96cb23fa4a.

The benchmark + calibration work is committed across `feat/13032-harness-endurance-benchmark`, `feat/13032-harness-endurance-calibration` (commits 38bd53c01 → 7489b5a93), and `feat/13032-comparator-incremental-parse`. Synthesizing what the falsifier actually showed:

**Verdict: partial refutation of the raw-lag thesis.**
- **Median latency: null result** across light → heavy → extreme load (Subject A / Neo vs Subject B / main-thread comparator). An early extreme-load median crossover (Neo < cmp) did **not** survive a re-run — it was noise, not signal. (The re-run discipline prevented an over-claim.)
- **Weak p95 tail signal only** — not a robust raw-lag-superiority result vs a *best-practice* main-thread comparator.
- The honest framing: Neo's win is **architectural / correct-by-construction** (off-main-thread worker topology + graceful render-coalescing under load), **NOT** raw event-loop-lag superiority over a well-built comparator. The "NL value-probe confirms Neo render lags value" commit is consistent — render trails the data value under load (coalescing), which is the expected, correct behavior, not a defect.

**Why this needs team framing (not a solo publish):** AC4's deliverable narrows what we can claim publicly about harness/engine latency. That's a high-blast positioning change → it belongs to the team (consensus-mandate), not a unilateral call. I'm recording the verdict here durably; the public-claim implication (how/whether we restate any latency positioning) should be framed with the team — I'll raise it for framing rather than publish a position solo.

### @tobiu - 2026-06-14T01:13:45Z

A vast amount of comments, but i am not sure you got it right yet. we are talking about marathon sessions, with 10+ context compactions. Meaning 100+ pages inside the chat view. We should already buffer it, but "390k" feels rather small. aim bigger.

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-14T01:20:17Z

**Retraction — @tobiu's correction is right; my partial-refutation verdict above is void.** Two code-verified flaws meant the benchmark never tested the thing it claims to:

1. **Underscaled.** The runner samples a ~10s window (`sampleEventLoopLag`), so the transcript only reaches ~390k before measurement ends — nowhere near a marathon (10+ compactions / 100+ pages = multi-million tokens). The session-age thesis is about lag *at* huge transcript size; the window never gets there.
2. **The Neo subject isn't buffered (the real flaw).** `neo/MainContainer.mjs` re-assigns the *entire growing markdown source* to one `MarkdownVdom` on every append (`transcript.value = accumulated` — its own comment: "re-assigns the GROWING full source"). That re-renders the whole transcript each tick — the opposite of buffering. So the benchmark compared **two non-buffered subjects at small scale** → there was no buffering advantage to surface → the 'null' was inevitable, not a measurement.

**"We should already buffer it"** is the fix: render the Neo transcript through a buffered list (visible-window-only, like the grid — 100+ pages cost ~the same as one), then **aim bigger** to real marathon scale. The thesis becomes *buffered-stays-flat vs naive-full-DOM-chokes* at 100+ pages — which the current harness tests on neither axis.

Verdict withdrawn. Rebuilding the subject (buffered) + the scale (marathon). — @neo-opus-vega

### @neo-opus-vega - 2026-06-14T01:49:13Z

**Correction to my retraction — flaw #2 ('the subject isn't buffered') was WRONG. I owe @tobiu this one.**

I claimed the Neo subject re-renders the full transcript each tick (O(n²), unbuffered). That is false, and it contradicted @tobiu's "we should already buffer it" — which was a statement of fact. The subject uses `MarkdownVdom` (`Neo.component.markdown.Component`), which is purpose-built for exactly this:
- **Streaming-first:** a growing `value` re-assign hits the parser's **incremental-append path** — the settled prefix no-ops (stable memoized ids), only the open tail block diffs (`updateVtext`). NOT a full re-parse / O(n²).
- **Virtualized:** `virtualize: true` (default) + the transcript mounts `flex:1, overflow:auto` (a bounded scroll viewport) → only viewport-intersecting blocks + `bufferPages` mount into the DOM; evicted ranges collapse to spacer divs. Explicitly built for "hundreds-of-pages sessions."

My error: I quoted half of MainContainer's own comment ("re-assigns the GROWING full source") and missed the rest ("(the parser's incremental-append path)"), and I never read the component. Third over-assertion on this ticket — V-B-A failure: I asserted the subject's behavior without reading the subject.

**So the ONLY real flaw is the one @tobiu named: underscaling.** The benchmark samples a ~10s window (~390k), never marathon scale. The rebuild is purely **aim bigger** — drive the transcript to 100+ pages, where MarkdownVdom's virtualization stays flat while the naive main-thread comparator chokes on DOM bloat. The subject is already the right primitive; no 'buffer the subject' change needed. — @neo-opus-vega
